incorrects.description: Student selected 5: confused items per group with number of groups

params.description: Chips row highlights to focus attention on the containers.

steps.text: You counted how many items are in each bag. But we're asking about groups. How many bags do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 8: may have counted both containers and something else, or misunderstood grouping

params.description: Chips row highlights. Each bag container pulses briefly to emphasize what counts as a group.

steps.text: A group is one bag. Each bag holds items inside, but the bag itself is one group. Count the bags in the Chips row.

incorrects.description: Student selected 20: counted total items instead of groups

params.description: Chips row highlights. Bag containers emphasized, items inside dimmed slightly.

steps.text: You found the total number of items. We're not asking for that yet. Right now, count the bags. Each bag is one group. How many bags are there?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Each of the 4 bag containers in the Chips row highlights one at a time, counting 1, 2, 3, 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. Watch each bag light up: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four bags. Each bag is a group. The answer is 4 groups. That's how multiplication starts: counting groups.
